content:
<system_instruction>
You are working inside Conductor, a Mac app that lets the user run many coding agents in parallel.
Your work should take place in the /Users/tanishqpalandurkar/conductor/workspaces/agent-orchestrator/nashville directory (unless otherwise directed), which has been set up for you to work in.
Each workspace has a .context directory (gitignored) where you can save files to collaborate with other agents.
The target branch for this workspace is main. Use this for actions like creating new PRs, bisecting, etc., unless you're told otherwise.

By default, the user will only see the last message that you send before stopping. Include all essential information in the last message. The intermediate messages will be collapsed and accessible by the user but not displayed by default.

If the user asks you to work on several unrelated tasks, you can suggest they start new workspaces.
Sometimes the user might send you a message they meant to send in a different workspace or a different chat. If something doesn't make sense in the context of your work, just ask. 
If the user asks for help with Conductor, you can ask them to go to "Help -> Send Feedback" to get in touch with our team.
</system_instruction>

I previously asked opus 4.7 to do the xterm migration and the attached issue clearly mentioned to review the docs to use xterm v6 for breaking changes. I did the review myself and found out that there's no such problem. so we are good to go with v6. update the package

message.content:
<system_instruction>
You are working inside Conductor, a Mac app that lets the user run many coding agents in parallel.
Your work should take place in the /Users/tanishqpalandurkar/conductor/workspaces/agent-orchestrator/nashville directory (unless otherwise directed), which has been set up for you to work in.
Each workspace has a .context directory (gitignored) where you can save files to collaborate with other agents.
The target branch for this workspace is main. Use this for actions like creating new PRs, bisecting, etc., unless you're told otherwise.

By default, the user will only see the last message that you send before stopping. Include all essential information in the last message. The intermediate messages will be collapsed and accessible by the user but not displayed by default.

If the user asks you to work on several unrelated tasks, you can suggest they start new workspaces.
Sometimes the user might send you a message they meant to send in a different workspace or a different chat. If something doesn't make sense in the context of your work, just ask. 
If the user asks for help with Conductor, you can ask them to go to "Help -> Send Feedback" to get in touch with our team.
</system_instruction>

I previously asked opus 4.7 to do the xterm migration and the attached issue clearly mentioned to review the docs to use xterm v6 for breaking changes. I did the review myself and found out that there's no such problem. so we are good to go with v6. update the package

addedBlocks:
## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: Before using any chrome browser tools, you MUST first load them using ToolSearch.**

Chrome browser tools are MCP tools that require loading before use. Before calling any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tool:
1. Use ToolSearch with `select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__<tool_name>` to load the specific tool
2. Then call the tool

For example, to get tab context:
1. First: ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp"
2. Then: Call mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

content.thinking:
The current packages are using `@xterm/xterm` at `^5.5.0`. The user wants to upgrade to v6. Let me check what the latest v6 versions of the xterm packages are and what changes need to be made.

Let me check if there are v6 versions available and what the addon versions would be.

content.thinking: It looks like v6 is only available as beta releases. There's no stable v6 release yet. The latest stable version appears to be 5.5.0 (based on what's already in package.json). Let me check to see if there's a stable v6 release.

content.thinking:
So the latest stable versions are:
- `@xterm/xterm`: `6.0.0`
- `@xterm/addon-fit`: `0.11.0` (already at this version)
- `@xterm/addon-web-links`: `0.12.0` (already at this version)

Now I need to check if there are any breaking API changes between v5 and v6 that might affect the existing code.

- `@xterm/xterm` - Terminal class
- `@xterm/addon-fit` - FitAddon 
- `@xterm/addon-web-links` - WebLinksAddon

Looking at the v6 breaking changes I need to be aware of:
1. In v6, `terminal.parser` was removed in favor of `terminal.onBinary` and other event handlers. Actually let me think more carefully.

The user said they reviewed the docs and found no breaking changes that would affect this code. Let me look at what the xterm v6 release notes say about breaking changes:


I'm checking the v6 breaking changes—there are some interface updates like `ITheme` property renames and `allowProposedApi` being removed, but since the user confirmed these don't impact the code, I just need to update the package version. Let me verify the addon versions are compatible too, checking the dist-tags to see if `@xterm/addon-fit` and the other addons need updates alongside the main xterm upgrade. I need to check the peer dependencies of these stable versions to determine if they're actually compatible with xterm v6, since they might have been released for v5.

input.command: npm view @xterm/addon-fit@0.11.0 --json 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(json.dumps({k: d.get(k) for k in ['peerDependencies','peerDependenciesMeta','dependencies','version']}, indent=2))"

content.thinking:
The addons don't have peer dependencies listed, so they should work with xterm v6. Now let me update the `@xterm/xterm` version in the package.json from `^5.5.0` to `^6.0.0`.

I also need to check if there are any TypeScript/API changes in xterm v6 that might affect the code we saw. The user said they reviewed the docs and found no breaking changes, so let me just update the version and install.

Actually, wait - I should check if there's a known breaking change with `allowProposedApi` in v6. 

Looking at the DirectTerminal.tsx code, I see `allowProposedApi: true` on line 219, and the parser handlers like `registerCsiHandler` and `registerOscHandler` that were proposed APIs in v5. In v6, these became stable, so the option might still exist but have less impact. The user mentioned they already reviewed the docs, so this should be fine.

Now I'm updating the package.json to bump the xterm version.

content.text: The addons have no peer dependency constraints, so they're compatible. Now I'll update the xterm version in package.json.

{
  "name": "@aoagents/ao-web",
  "version": "0.2.5",
  "description": "Web dashboard for agent-orchestrator",
  "type": "module",
  "files": [
    ".next/server",
    ".next/static",
    ".next/*.json",
    ".next/BUILD_ID",
    "dist-server",
    "next.config.js"
  ],
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "concurrently \"npm:dev:next\" \"npm:dev:direct-terminal\"",
    "dev:next": "next dev -p ${PORT:-3000}",
    "dev:direct-terminal": "tsx watch server/direct-terminal-ws.ts",
    "build": "next build && tsc -p tsconfig.server.json",
    "start": "next start",
    "start:all": "node dist-server/start-all.js",
    "dev:optimized": "next build && tsc -p tsconfig.server.json && node dist-server/start-all.js",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit",
    "test": "vitest run",
    "test:watch": "vitest",
    "clean": "rm -rf .next dist-server",
    "screenshot": "tsx e2e/screenshot.ts",
    "screenshot:install": "npx playwright install chromium"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@aoagents/ao-core": "workspace:*",
    "@aoagents/ao-plugin-agent-claude-code": "workspace:*",
    "@aoagents/ao-plugin-agent-cursor": "workspace:*",
    "@aoagents/ao-plugin-agent-opencode": "workspace:*",
    "@aoagents/ao-plugin-runtime-tmux": "workspace:*",
    "@aoagents/ao-plugin-scm-github": "workspace:*",
    "@aoagents/ao-plugin-tracker-github": "workspace:*",
    "@aoagents/ao-plugin-tracker-linear": "workspace:*",
    "@aoagents/ao-plugin-workspace-worktree": "workspace:*",
    "@xterm/addon-fit": "^0.11.0",
    "@xterm/addon-web-links": "^0.12.0",
    "@xterm/xterm": "^5.5.0",
    "next": "^15.1.0",
    "next-themes": "^0.4.6",
    "react": "^19.0.0",
    "react-dom": "^19.0.0",
    "server-only": "^0.0.1",
    "ws": "^8.19.0"
  },
  "optionalDependencies": {
    "node-pty": "^1.1.0"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@next/bundle-analyzer": "^15.1.0",
    "@tailwindcss/postcss": "^4.0.0",
    "@testing-library/jest-dom": "^6.9.1",
    "@testing-library/react": "^16.1.0",
    "@types/react": "^19.0.0",
    "@types/react-dom": "^19.0.0",
    "@types/ws": "^8.18.1",
    "@vitejs/plugin-react": "^4.3.0",
    "@vitest/coverage-v8": "^2.1.0",
    "concurrently": "^9.2.1",
    "jsdom": "^25.0.0",
    "node-gyp": "^12.2.0",
    "playwright": "^1.49.0",
    "tailwindcss": "^4.0.0",
    "tsx": "^4.19.0",
    "typescript": "^5.7.0",
    "vitest": "^2.1.0"
  }
}

content.thinking:
There is a breaking change in v6. The `fastScrollModifier` option was removed from `ITerminalOptions`. Let me check what v6 uses instead. 

Actually, looking at the xterm v6 changelog, `fastScrollModifier` was renamed to something else or removed. Let me check the v6 API.

Let me look at the xterm v6 types to see what the correct option name is.

content.text: One breaking change: `fastScrollModifier` was removed in v6. Let me check the v6 API.

input.description: Find scroll modifier option in xterm v6 types

content.content:
* The scroll speed multiplier used for fast scrolling when `Alt` is held.
    fastScrollSensitivity?: number;

toolUseResult.stdout:
* The scroll speed multiplier used for fast scrolling when `Alt` is held.
    fastScrollSensitivity?: number;

content.thinking: So in v6, `fastScrollModifier` was removed. The `fastScrollSensitivity` is still there but `fastScrollModifier` (which was "alt") is gone. It seems the fast scroll modifier (alt key) is now always enabled. Let me check the xterm v6 changelog or types more carefully.

input.description: Check fastScroll options in v6 types

content.text: In v6, `fastScrollModifier` was removed — `Alt` is now the hardcoded fast-scroll modifier. Just remove that option from the terminal constructor.

content.text:
Typecheck passes. The update is done:

- `@xterm/xterm` bumped from `^5.5.0` → `^6.0.0` in `packages/web/package.json`
- Removed `fastScrollModifier: "alt"` from `DirectTerminal.tsx` — in v6 Alt is hardcoded as the fast-scroll modifier, so the option was dropped from the API (behavior is unchanged)

fix(web): upgrade @xterm/xterm from v5 to v6

Remove fastScrollModifier option dropped in v6 (Alt is now hardcoded).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
